request.question: A-ha was founded by the keyboardest who was of what nationality?

title: Order of Fidelity
Magne Furuholmen
George Willcox
Federation of Gay Games
A-ha
International Boy Scouts, Troop 1
Liberian nationality law
List of A-ha tours

ECUARUNARI
sentences: The Order of Fidelity (Urdhëri Besa), also known as Order of Besa, was founded by King Zog I when he was President of Albania.
Founded on 22 December 1926, it was initially awarded in four classes (1.
Grand Cordon with star-Kordon i Madh me Yll 2.
Grand Officer-Oficer i Madh, 3.
Commander-Komandar, and 4.
Knight-Kalorës) and a medal, and was limited to six ordinary recipients of Albanian nationality, and not awardable to foreigners.
It was remodelled in 1932 and reduced to three classes (1.
Grand Cordon with star-Kordon i Madh me Yll 2.
Commander-Komandar, and 3.
Knight-Kalorës) and remained to be limited to only six ordinary recipients of Albanian nationality, and not made awardable to foreigners,
Magne Furuholmen (born 1 November 1962) is a Norwegian musician and visual artist.

Furuholmen, better known to music fans by his stage name Mags, is the keyboardist of the synthpop/rock band A-ha, and co-wrote many of the band's hits including "Take on Me", "Stay on These Roads", "Manhattan Skyline", "Cry Wolf", "Forever Not Yours", "Analogue (All I Want)", and "Foot of the Mountain".
George Willcox (born 1949) is an archaeobotanist who specialises in the origins of agriculture in the Near East.
He has been employed as researcher with the CNRS (Centre national de la recherche scientifique)France since 1989.
He has dual French and British nationality.

He studied environmental archaeology under the late Prof. Dimbleby at the Institute of Archaeology in London during the early 1970s and was then was employed as environmental archaeologist by the Museum of London.
In 1977 he married and left his job to follow his interest in Near Eastern archaeobotany.
An interest which he had acquired as an undergraduate working with Gordon Hillman in Turkey.

During the next few years he worked in Turkey, Afghanistan Jordan, Oman, Kuwait, Tajikistan, India and Syria and started amassing a charred archaeobotanical collection and modern reference collection.
These are now housed at the Muséum national d’Histoire naturelle in Paris.
It was during this somewhat precarious period that he moved to France and founded a family.

In 1984 Gordon Hillman introduced George to Jacques Cauvin and Patrica Anderson at the CNRS Institut de Préhistore Orientale in south east France where they developed a project on experimentally cultivating wild cereals In 1989 George entered the CNRS working on a number of early Neolithic sites in Syria in particular with Danielle Stordeur at Jerf el Amhar.
He lectured at several French universities on archaeobotany and the origins of agriculture and supervised post graduate students.
He was an associate editor for Vegetation History and Archaeobotany for many years and received an honorary doctorate from the University of Basel in 2013.
His 117 scholarly publications are a measure of his research achievements.

The Federation of Gay Games (FGG) is the sanctioning body of the Gay Games which was founded in 1982 by Tom Waddell, after he dealt first-hand with the prejudice towards gay athletes and their inability to openly participate in sporting events.
The Federation of Gay Games hosts the world's biggest cultural and sporting event for the LGBT community every four years.

The Federation of Gay Games is founded on the principles of "participation, inclusion, and personal best" and continues to support the LGBT community not only through the Gay Games but through scholarships for underprivileged members.
The Federation of Gay Games continues to expand throughout the world and accepts members of any sexual orientation and nationality to participate in the games or contribute.
The FGG is proudly hosting the next Gay Games in 2018 in Paris, France.
A-ha (usually stylized as a"-h"a; ] ) is a Norwegian band formed in Oslo in 1982.
The band was founded by Morten Harket (vocals), Magne Furuholmen (keyboards) and Pål Waaktaar-Savoy (guitars).
The group rose to fame during the mid-1980s after being discovered by musician and producer John Ratcliff, and continued global success in the 1990s and 2000s.

International Boy Scouts, Troop 1 was founded in 1911 as Japan's first Scout troop and, despite its early multinational character, was originally registered with the London headquarters of the Boy Scouts as "British Scouts in Foreign Countries", which was the only option at the time due to the Boy Scouts' "nationality requirement" and no international Boy Scout office.
The troop's character, and Scoutmaster, changed and it was recognized as a mixed-nationality, or "international" troop in 1918.
After formation of the World Scout Bureau, Troop 1 became the first "mixed nationality" group to be registered directly be the Bureau.
The troop has been continuously active since its foundation.

The troop's "mixed nationality" charter dated October 30, 1925 was issued after the newly formed International Bureau received approval to directly register such "international" groups at the 3rd World Scout Conference.
The charter was signed by Lord Baden-Powell.
The Republic of Liberia was founded by African slaves from North America by the American Colonization Society and returned to establish a republic on African soil.
Nationality law is given in the Aliens and Nationality Law of 1973, based on its 1847 Constitution.
Human rights activists have described current citizenship laws as explicitly racist, as it requires being black as a prerequisite to citizenship.
The first constitution allowed for women to transmit their nationality to their children, although multiple citizenship was not permitted nor is it permitted in revisions of the constitution.
A-ha is a Norwegian synthpop act, founded in 1982 by Morten Harket, Magne Furuholmen and Paul Waaktaar-Savoy in Oslo.
Since 1985, A-ha has performed throughout the world, including tours in North America, South America, Europe, Australia, Japan, and China.

Mintohren, also Mintōren, is a grass-roots movement created by young Zainichi Koreans to combat ethnic discrimination by encouraging a multicultural and positive approach to integration of Koreans into Japanese society.

The movement started in the 1970s as a joint effort of three groups: Mukuge no Kai, founded by Lee Kyung-jae in 1972 in Osaka, Tokkabi Kodomo Kai, founded in 1974 in Osaka, and Seikyu Sha, founded in 1974 in Tokyo.

In 1985, they began a campaign in Takatsuki, Osaka Prefecture which resulted in the establishment of a division in the city board of education devoted exclusively to education for ethnic Koreans, and the elimination of the requirement that city employees hold Japanese nationality.

They also played an important role in the 1980s struggle against the requirement that all foreign residents, including Special Permanent Residents like the Zainichi Koreans, give their fingerprints to police; they organised refusers from different parts of Japan, as well as providing information and increasing awareness of their campaign.

ECUARUNARI (in Kichwa: "Ecuador Runakunapak Rikcharimuy ", "Movement of the indigenous people of Ecuador"), also known as Confederation of Peoples of Kichwa Nationality ("Ecuador Kichwa Llaktakunapak Jatun Tantanakuy ," in Spanish "Confederación de Pueblos de la Nacionalidad Kichwa del Ecuador ") is the organization of indigenous peoples of Kichwa nationality in the Ecuadorian central mountain region, founded in 1972.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- If summary_1 already includes explicit profession/role/descriptor tokens for an anchor (for example, 'singer', 'director', 'country music artist'), omit those redundant descriptor tokens and emit a compact anchor-only query consisting of the core visible anchors (names, titles, groups). Retain relation tokens only when the question explicitly requests them or when disambiguation rules demand them. Do not remove descriptors when the question explicitly asks for role/occupation evidence or when a short-title disambiguation rule requires adding an entertainment disambiguator.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity (or otherwise names candidate/bridge entities), treat the context claim as the summary anchor and perform explicit identity-alignment checks before using passage facts. For each entity explicitly named in `context` as a candidate or bridge, do the following: if at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append a single brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that linking fact and references the corroborating passage and also include one short clause for that context-named entity giving the passage-supported relation value (e.g., "Name: [relation value]."); if no passage provides the relation value for that context-named entity, append a short clause stating that no relation value is provided in the evidence (e.g., "Name: no [relation] provided in evidence."). Do not create such clauses for entities that are only mentioned in `passages` and are not named in `context`. Do not append corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep these additions short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.
- Vocal-role single-token normalization (narrow scope): If summary_2 (alone or composed with summary_1 where identity links are explicit) explicitly contains vocal-role evidence — i.e., one or more verbalized vocal-role phrases that clearly denote a singing performer (examples: "lead singer", "lead vocals", "vocalist" as presented in the summaries) — then you may prefer to emit the concise single-token common noun for a person who sings (e.g., "singer") only when that single-token common noun itself appears verbatim in the summaries or when the vocal-role phrases in the summaries unambiguously denote the same person-role. If those explicit lexical licenses are not present, retain the literal role phrasing as given in summary_2 and do not normalize modifiers or drop qualifiers.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: A-ha was founded by the keyboardest who was of what nationality?
hop1_docs: Chilean National Zoo | The Chilean National Zoo ("Zoológico Nacional de Chile") is a 4.8 ha zoo that was founded in 1925 in Santiago, Chile.  Located at the foot of San Cristóbal Hill in what is known as the Santiago Metropolitan Park ("Parque Metropolitano de Santiago"), the zoo is home to thousands of animals representing 158 species.  Unique exhibits feature Chilean native animals and birds including rare and endangered species.

Big Gus, What's the Fuss? | Big Gus, What's the Fuss?  (also known as Ha-Balash Ha'Amitz Shvartz and Fat Spy) was a 1973 Israeli comedy film directed by Ami Artzi and American director Lloyd Kaufman, the president of Troma Entertainment.  The plot revolves around a Hebrew detective named Gus and his partner Harry who are hired by a woman to investigate a possible case of adultery.

Hamra National Park | Hamra National Park (Swedish: "Hamra nationalpark" ) is a national park in Ljusdal Municipality, Gävleborg County, Sweden.  The park, which is a part of Orsa Finnmark (part of Dalarna), was founded in 1909 and was then 28 ha in area, but was extended in 2011 to 1383 ha .

Ha Hee-ra | Ha Hee-ra (born October 30, 1969) is a South Korean actress.  She was born in Seoul to a Taiwanese immigrant father and a Korean mother.  Ha began her acting career in 1981 when she was in the sixth grade, appearing mostly in films for teenagers.  In the 1990s, Ha became one of the top actresses in Korean television, along with Chae Shi-ra and Kim Hee-ae.  Among her notable dramas include "What Women Want" (1990), "What Is Love" (1991), "The Break of Dawn" (1993), "Catching Up with Gangnam Moms" (2007) and "President" (2010).

Murrumbidgee Valley National Park | The Murrumbidgee Valley National Park is a protected national park that is located in the Riverina region of New South Wales, in eastern Australia.  The 47703 ha national park is located approximately 6 km east of Hay , and approximately 15 km west of Narrandera .  The park protects part of what is now the largest continuous tract of river red gum forest in the world.

Dark Blue Dream | Dark Blue Dream is the second studio album by composer and keyboardest Steve Weingart.  Produced by Weingart, the album features guitarist Frank Gambale, bassist Jimmy Earl and drummer Tom Brechtlein.  The album was released worldwide in 2006 by Skeewa Music.

Isomgosiono | Isomgosiono, "Queen Anne", 1703 Treaty Signer for Pan NA Federation along with Sachem George Skipper of the Nottoway/Chero-en-ha-ka/Iroquois.  These pan Native American communities go back to what may be communities formed per the one reference on the Capt John Smith Map of 1656 listing a single Shawnee group in the Susquahanna Valley when he mapped the area at that time.  This map is found in the appendix of the Capt. John Smith Trail of the National Colonial Park System.  The parent of Queen Anne is of the Shawnee of that mapped valley and they had been brought over by Pardo in 1570, according to carbon dating of their settlement area (Langston-MOretti, National Park Service, Ch.  4, Archaeology Study ) along with the Old Cheraw, after Pardo conquered the 20,000 Wichita, Kansas Iowans who lived there.  Of those who were left, some were enslaved as "Monocan" (means Hemp Grower in Chiwere) and brought to the Susquahanna. (NPS Langston-Moretti, Ch.  4 and 10 of Weromicococo State Park/National Colonial Park Archaeology Study).  The Cheraw were listed as Xualla by Pardo, Sara by the Tazewell, TN group found by Pardo, and Charra by cartographers of the blended 1724 encampment of surviving small pox plaqued tribes who had to put aside their differences who would not normally have lived near each other.
response.summary_1: Passage evidence: "Dark Blue Dream is the second studio album by composer and keyboardest Steve Weingart" (passage 6). Missing/bridge: no passage links Steve Weingart to A-ha, and none identify A-ha's founding keyboardist or state that person's nationality. Next hop direction: retrieve A-ha's founding keyboardist (search "A-ha founding keyboardist" or "Magne Furuholmen A-ha") and then retrieve that person's nationality (search "Magne Furuholmen nationality" or "nationality of A-ha keyboardist").
hop2_docs: Hamra National Park | Hamra National Park (Swedish: "Hamra nationalpark" ) is a national park in Ljusdal Municipality, Gävleborg County, Sweden.  The park, which is a part of Orsa Finnmark (part of Dalarna), was founded in 1909 and was then 28 ha in area, but was extended in 2011 to 1383 ha .

Gwenn ha du (Breton paramilitary group) | Gwenn ha Du (Breton for "white and black") was a paramilitary group founded at the end of 1930 in Paris by Célestin Lainé.  It advocated Breton nationalism through "direct action" and published a secret manual aimed at instructing readers in terrorism.  The phrase "Gwenn ha du" is the nickname for the Breton flag, which is in these colours.

Parque Nacional Del Este | The Parque Nacional Del Este (or National Park of the East) is located in eastern Dominican Republic.  Founded on September 16, 1975, the park consists of 31,244 Ha of open space on the southeastern peninsula of Hispaniola and 10,650 Ha on Isla Saona, a popular tourist destination, totalling 41,894 Ha in protected area.  The protected area also includes a 12,000 Ha buffer zone around the park boundaries.

Apparatjik | Apparatjik ( ) are a multi-national band formed in 2008.  The band is a supergroup that consists of bassist Guy Berryman from Coldplay, guitarist/keyboardist Magne Furuholmen from A-ha, singer/guitarist Jonas Bjerre of Mew and drummer/producer Martin Terefe.  The band's name literal translation is "agent of the apparatus".  Apparatjik is based on apparatchik, which is a word of Russian origin and is used to describe "an official or bureaucrat in any organization" but has in the past had other meanings, including "Communist agent or spy".

National Parks of Ukraine | National nature parks of Ukraine are preservation territories that are part of the Nature-Preservation Fund of Ukraine.  The total area protected by national parks is approximately 1111600 ha , for an average of 22685 ha but a median of only 14836 ha at Zalissya.  The largest national park is Upper Pobozhia in Khmelnytskyi Oblast: at over 100000 ha .  The smallest park is Derman-Ostroh National Nature Park, at less than 5500 ha .

Utrechtse Heuvelrug National Park | Utrechtse Heuvelrug National Park is a national park in the Dutch province of Utrecht.  When it was founded in 2003, the park covered 6000 ha of heathlands, shifting sands, forests, grass lands and floodplains.  In 2013 the park was extended to 10000 ha , adding the area north of highway A12 when the ecoduct "Mollebos" was realized.  The most striking landscape feature is the glacial ridge after which the park is named.
Baba Nation | Baba Nation was a Norwegian soul/funk band, founded in the city of Bergen by Jan Erik de Lange Gullaksen in 1990, then by the name Yee ’Ha Wanna Have A Baba.
